INTERPRETATION
• Although no mutations or gene fusions were identified, overexpression of genes associated with thyroid cancer was identified in this sample.
• Based on the results of the multicenter validation study, FNA samples with abnormal gene expression profile were associated with -60% probability of cancer, typically low-risk papillary carcinoma, or NIFTP.
• Correlation with nodule size, imaging and other clinical data is recommended to define the most appropriate patient management.

INTERPRETATION
EIF1AX splice-site mutation was identified in this sample at a high level, which is associated with ~60%
probability of cancer or NIFTP, whereas the remaining nodules are clonal follicular adenomas (PMID:
35609001).
The majority of EIF1AX splice-site mutation-positive nodules are low-risk cancer or NIFTP. Therefore, if
surgical management is planned, thyroid lobectomy may be considered in appropriately selected patients.

INTERPRETATION
• The performed analysis was negative for all tested genetic alterations associated with thyroid cancer.
• Based on the results of prospective multicenter validation study, the probability of cancer in thyroid nodules with Bethesda III-IV cytology and negative ThyroSeg GC result is -3%. However, in large-size nodules, the results of molecular testing should be interpreted with caution and in close correlation with imaging and ether clinical findings, because limited FNA sampling of a large nodule may increase the likelihood of a false-negative test result.

INTERPRETATION
• PAX8-GLIS3 fusion was identified in this sample.
• Recent studies showed that PAX8-GLISS fusion is a common finding in thyroid hyalinizing trabecular tumors, whereas they were not detected in a large series of papillary thyroid carcinomas (Nikiforova MN et al. Thyroid. 2019, 29:161-173; MarchiA? C et al. Mod Pathol. 2019). However, a single case of papillary thyroid carcinoma positive for this fusion has been reported (Yoo SK, et al. PLos Genet 2016, 12(8):e1006239).
• Although the available data are limited, the probability of papillary cancer in nodules with PAX8-GLIS3 is expected to be low, and most of these nodules are likely to be essentially benign hyalinizing trabecular tumors,

Multiple intermediate-level chromosomal copy number alterations were identified in this sample.

appropriately selected patients.

• Low level KRAS mutations are usually found in benign thyroid nodules, with only 5-10% probability of typically low-risk cancer if removed at present time. • In the absence of concerning clinical and ultrasound findings, active surveillance may be considered with repeat of clinical exam within 1 year.

·          In the absence of high-risk mutations (such as TERT, TP53), these tumors, if cancers, are expected to be of intermediate risk for recurrence. Correlation with imaging and other clinical data is recommended.

INTERPRETATION
• No mutations with well-established thyroid cancer association were identified in this sample.
• In addition, an EZH1 mutation was found at a high level. EZH1 mutations typically occur in benign and frequently autonomously functioning thyroid nodules. Therefore, it is most likely that at present time the sampled nodule is benign. However, the presence of a clonal mutation found at a high level indicates that the nodule is a neoplasm, not hyperplasia. With time, some of these nodules may undergo progression and acquire additional transforming molecular alterations.
• Correlation with imaging and other clinical and laboratory data is recommended. In the absence of suspicious ultrasonographic and other clinical findings, active surveillance may be considered with repeating clinical exam within 1 year in lieu of surgical excision.
• Of note, a RET mutation (p.V804M) was identified in this sample at an allele frequency near 50%. Given this allele frequency, the mutation is more likely to be germline, although this test cannot discriminate between germline (inherited) and sporadic (acquired) mutations, which can be reliably done by testing patient blood. If clinical suspicion for a familial disease exists, genetic counseling and additional testing may be considered. Assuming that this mutation is germline, it is not expected to alter the above probability of cancer/NIFTP in the sampled nodule.

typically a late event associated with more aggressive tumor behavior.

histologically as follicular adenomas which should be carefully examined for invasion.

appropriately selected patients.

INTERPRETATION
DICER1 mutation was identified in this case.
The presence of a clonal DICER1 mutation indicates that the sampled nodule is a neoplasm,
typically follicular adenoma, whereas ~30% of those are cancers or non-invasive follicular
thyroid neoplasm with papillary-like nuclear features (NIFTP).
Most of these cancers are expected to be of low risk for recurrence, although rare cases of
poorly differentiated carcinoma in nodules positive for DICER1 have been reported in children
and young adults (Chernock RD et al. Mod Pathol. 2020, PMID: 31937902).
In the absence of worrisome ultrasonographic and clinical findings, thyroid lobectomy may be
considered in appropriately selected patients.
This hotspot DICER1 mutation is expected to be somatic, but in some cases it co-exists with
another, germline mutation located in the different area of the gene (Wasserman JD et al.
JCEM, 2018). This test does not sequence the entire DICER1 gene and therefore most of
germline DICER1 mutations would not be detected. If clinical suspicion for DICER1 syndrome
exists, genetic counseling may be considered.
Patient management decisions must be based on the independent medical judgment of the
treating physician. Molecular test results should be taken into consideration in conjunction with
all relevant imaging and clinical findings, patient and family history, as well as patient
preference.

• RET fusions are associated with a very high probability of cancer, typically papillary thyroid carcinoma.
